Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a significant global threat, with inadequate diagnosis contributing to antimicrobial misuse. India urgently needs high-quality, affordable diagnostic tools. Despite advances in molecular techniques for identifying pathogens and resistance genes, a lack of standardized validation frameworks has hindered their adoption. To address this, the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), in collaboration with the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO), has developed a comprehensive protocol for validating rapid diagnostic tests for pathogen identification and antimicrobial susceptibility testing. This protocol aims to guide developers, expedite validation, reduce regulatory approval times, and facilitate the timely introduction of validated tests in the Indian healthcare system to combat AMR.

Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a critical public health challenge exacerbated by delayed diagnosis and lack of affordable diagnostic tools. In India, where infectious diseases are a significant burden, the absence of a standardized framework for validating advanced diagnostic tests has hindered their adoption. To address this, the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) and the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO), with stakeholders, have developed a comprehensive validation protocol for rapid diagnostic tests. This protocol provides a systematic approach for evaluating diagnostic tools against regulatory standards, aiming to foster collaboration and introduce effective diagnostics to combat AMR in India.

The validation process is divided into three phases: Development, Validation, and Maintenance. The Development Phase establishes purpose and objectives, standardizes reagents and protocols, and implements quality control and assurance. The Validation Phase involves creating standard operating procedures (SOPs) based on study design and testing performance parameters such as reproducibility, accuracy, precision, stability, and analytical/diagnostic characteristics. The Maintenance Phase focuses on extending and upholding validation criteria through ongoing monitoring and proficiency testing, culminating in a comprehensive dossier of validation.

The development of a new IVD prototype progresses through TRL 1-8. TRL 1-4 involves the initial prototype and obtaining an MD-13 license for domestic manufacture. TRL 5-6 encompasses pre-clinical validation (assessing analytical sensitivity, specificity, reproducibility, and accuracy) followed by an MD-25 license for clinical performance evaluation, then clinical validation (evaluating clinical validity, utility, sensitivity, and specificity). Finally, TRL 7-8 requires an MD-29 license for clinical data approval, leading to manufacturing for sale under MD-5/MD-6 (SLA) and MD-9/MD-10 (CLA).
General considerations for validation of diagnostics
This document outlines the criteria for developing and validating new rapid diagnostic assays for identifying pathogens and conducting antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST).

An applicant submits their application with requisite documents and fees. The submitted documents are then reviewed. If any deficiency is found, a query is raised. If all documents are in order, they are considered satisfactory, leading to approval.

The process for In Vitro Diagnostic (IVD) applications begins with a one-time applicant registration, populating user and manufacturer profiles, selecting the application type, and undertaking to provide correct information. Applicants then detail manufacturer and IVD product specifics, upload checklist documents and payment challan, and submit the application. The submitted application is received by a Nodal Officer, forwarded to a Reviewing Officer, and then to DDA/L+. Any deficiencies trigger a query requiring resubmission by the applicant; otherwise, the application proceeds to the Licensing Authority for final approval.
